scenario: North Logistics handles 6000 shipments per day in Camunda 8.8. A subprocess contains 3 parallel branches that produce work in progress. When one branch detects a critical failure, every remaining branch inside the same subprocess must be cancelled and the subprocess must finish immediately.
question: Which end event variant ends every active token within the same scope and terminates that scope at once?

options.text: Terminate end event ends every token within its containing scope, completing the enclosing subprocess or process immediately
None end event ends every token within the surrounding scope at once and is the only end event with that semantics
Error end event ends every token within the surrounding scope after first publishing the configured error code to all subscribers
Signal end event ends every token within the surrounding scope after broadcasting the configured signal name to all subscribers

a.text: Correct. The Camunda 8 docs describe the terminate end event as ending all active executions in its containing scope and immediately completing that scope.

b.text: Incorrect. A none end event consumes only its own token; sibling branches continue to run until they reach their own end events.

c.text: Incorrect. Error end events throw an error that must be caught; they do not implicitly cancel the surrounding scope as a whole.

d.text: Incorrect. Signal end events broadcast a signal; they do not cancel sibling branches inside the same scope by themselves.

explanation: Terminate end events end every active execution inside the containing scope and complete that scope immediately, which fits a critical-branch cancellation pattern.
